title: Under the Bridge
special_element: Beneath the silt, within reach of where the creature rests, lie three generations of offerings — coins, buttons, a child's carved horse, a soldier's ring — every token anyone threw off the bridge to appease the haunting. The creature has arranged them in concentric rings, by size, oldest at the centre. It has been paying attention for a very long time.
summary: Stand at the middle of the Old Stone Bridge on a still night and listen past the water: under the current there is something else.
body: Stand at the middle of the Old Stone Bridge on a still night and listen past the water: under the current there is something else. A low, regular rhythm — not water over stone, but something drawing breath. The moss on the underside of the arch grows pale and thick as velvet, and anything left on the parapet overnight is gone by morning.

The bridge has been called haunted for three generations. Travelers hear footsteps that belong to no one. They feel watched from below. The sensible response — a coin over the rail, a muttered apology — has been the local habit for as long.
The coin never reaches the riverbed.

Something lives beneath the arch. It has lived there longer than the bridge has stood; the original cut stones were laid *over* it, the builders mistaking its stillness for bedrock. It is not a ghost. It is a living thing, vast and slow, that wears the river around itself the way a stone wears moss.
The footsteps are its movements. The feeling of being watched is accurate.

Special element: Beneath the silt, within reach of where the creature rests, lie three generations of offerings — coins, buttons, a child's carved horse, a soldier's ring — every token anyone threw off the bridge to appease the haunting. The creature has arranged them in concentric rings, by size, oldest at the centre. It has been paying attention for a very long time.
rumours: **Truth:** No ghost. A living creature — old, enormous, unhurried — has occupied the space below the arch since before the bridge existed. It is not malicious. It collects every offering thrown down and watches every traveler who crosses. It has never harmed anyone. Whether that is restraint, indifference, or something closer to fondness, no one has stayed long enough to learn.

**Rumours:** *"A mason fell during construction and was never found — that's your ghost."* (A mason did fall. The creature moved the body somewhere dry. It has not said where.) *"Throw a coin and you'll cross safe."* (Travelers believe this sincerely. Either the creature earned the belief, or it simply persists because no one who threw a coin has yet drowned.)

**How to reveal:** The coin-rings are visible to anyone who dives or uses a spell to see below the surface — too precise to be accidental. If the party lingers on the bridge past nightfall, a hand — slow, enormous, grey-green — rises just above the waterline, holds a moment, and slides back under. It does not reach for them. It is, as best anyone can tell, waving.

title: Marrow's Toll
special_element: The coins are not an offering — they are a tally. Each one that sinks counts toward the total that sets Old Marrow free. The river is not receiving tribute; it is keeping score.
body: Category: Secret · Tier: 2 · Mood: mysterious

Every fare Old Marrow collects goes into the river — not a purse, not a box, straight into the dark water before the far bank comes near. The sound is always the same: a soft knock against the hull, then nothing. No splash. The coin sinks without troubling the surface.

The crossing has been here longer than any road that leads to it. People on both banks say Old Marrow is a river-spirit in a man's shape, and the coins are tribute. Ask him, and you get a flat look and three words: *"The river keeps toll."*
He's not wrong. But the toll is not the river's to keep.

Old Marrow is working off a debt counted in fares. How many he has paid and how many remain, he no longer knows — the water swallowed the count long ago. He knows only this: when the tally is full, he goes free. What *free* means he has never said, because no one has asked the question that would make him.
Every unpaid fare leaves the tally a coin short. Old Marrow does not forget, and he has never yet had to collect. Patience this old is not the same thing as forgiveness.
Special element: The coins are not an offering — they are a tally. Each one that sinks counts toward the total that sets Old Marrow free. The river is not receiving tribute; it is keeping score.
rumours: **Truth:** Old Marrow is bound to the crossing by an old compact whose terms are his alone to know — but the mechanism is the tally. Every coin paid and dropped counts toward a total that may be vast; he is partway through it, and the water is the only ledger that holds the number. An unpaid fare does not vanish. It sits as a deficit he will collect in time, with interest measured in obligation, not coin.

**Rumours:** *He's a river-god, and the coins are sacrifice.* / *He's been out here so long the loneliness broke something in him.* / *Divers went down once and found thousands on the bottom — none rusted, every one face-up.*

**How to reveal:** Let it surface across meetings, not one. The toll going overboard is visible from the first scene. A second crossing, with a History or Insight check, catches that the ritual has a rhythm — not grief, not superstition, something like counting. A third meeting, or a PC who asks *"What happens when you've paid enough?"*, cracks it open. Old Marrow answers only the question asked, nothing more: *"Then I am done."*

title: The Saltwidow's One Memory
special_element: She finished it two years ago. The shells already hold every fragment she needs — she has simply never let herself lay them out in order, because a face stitched from a hundred strangers' glimpses might not be his, and as long as she keeps collecting, she never has to find out.
body: Category: Secret · Tier: 3 · Mood: melancholy

Behind the Saltwidow's shop is a room that smells of drying kelp and warm wax. A hundred small shells sit on the shelves in no order a customer could guess, and touching one makes it hum, faint as a held breath. She has never once let anyone count them.

Every favour the Saltwidow trades — a debt called in, a name whispered to the right ear, a rival quietly ruined — she converts, sooner or later, into a stolen memory. Not just any memory. She takes only fragments that graze the edges of what she is looking for: a child's laugh heard through a wall, a small hand gripping a larger one at low tide, the particular blue of a coat nobody else remembers the colour of.

She lost her own child to the sea, and the sea's price for almost giving him back was her memory of his face. What she has spent years assembling, shell by shell, favour by favour, is not a keepsake. It is a reconstruction — other people's half-glimpses of a boy who was never theirs, stitched into something she can hold up in the dark and call whole.
She tells herself she is still short. One more fragment, she says, and the mosaic completes.

Special element: She finished it two years ago. The shells already hold every fragment she needs — she has simply never let herself lay them out in order, because a face stitched from a hundred strangers' glimpses might not be his, and as long as she keeps collecting, she never has to find out.
rumours: **Truth:** The Saltwidow has everything she needs to reconstruct her son's face. She keeps trading favours for fragments she no longer requires, manufacturing debts to keep the ritual — and the delay — alive.
**Rumours:** Coastal talk holds she is one memory short, and that the last piece must come from someone who has lost a child themself. That rumour is the lie she wants told.

**What a PC already paid her:** If a character has sold her a memory of their own, this room is where it went — and it went onto a shelf she had already stopped needing to fill. What she took from the player was never the missing piece; it was another year of not looking.

**How to reveal:** Never state it outright. Let a sharp-eyed party notice the shells are never recounted, or that a fragment she "needed" duplicates one she already owns. If cornered, she will not confirm it — she will simply stop trading for the rest of the scene, which is confirmation enough.

**If the party lays out the shells:** She does not thank them. Whatever face assembles, she looks at it once and puts the shells back in no order at all. From that night on her price for any favour doubles, and she will not take memories in payment again — the only currency she ever wanted is now the one thing she cannot bear to be handed.

title: The Fifth Return
special_element: The fifth return has already happened. Somewhere ahead of this moment, one of the party will kneel over Aldous's body, press their hand into warm wax, and speak his name — and that act is what left the print sitting in the reliquary years before it happens. The loop isn't a threat approaching; it's a wound already made, folded backward through time. Aldous doesn't fear the fifth return because it might occur. He fears it because it's the one time he knows, with total certainty, that someone who loves him will be strong enough to bring him back — and weak enough not to let him stay dead.
body: Category: Secret · Tier: 4 · Mood: heroic

Ashwell Chapel keeps four hands of cold candle wax in a locked reliquary — each pressed from a living palm, each more melted and clawed than the last, as though the thing inside the wax had been straining to climb out. They are how the Order calls Ser Aldous the Unfallen back from the ground: one wax hand, and his name spoken by someone who loved him. There is a fifth hand in the case. No one has cast it. It is warm to the touch.

Ser Aldous the Unfallen has died four times, and four times the Order dragged him back, a little less himself with every return. The brothers who keep the chapel tell it as devotion: they love him too much to let him stay dead. They do not know — because Aldous has never told them — that he begs to be let go every time, in the moments before the wax sets.
He has stopped begging the brothers. He is begging the party instead.

The fifth hand is the thing none of them can explain. It was found already in the case eleven years ago, before Aldous's third death, let alone a fourth or fifth. No seer carved it. It is a physical object that should not exist yet — a hand-shape identical, whorl for whorl, to a palm no one at the chapel has ever pressed into wax.
It matches a hand the party will recognize, eventually, as one of their own.

Special element: The fifth return has already happened. Somewhere ahead of this moment, one of the party will kneel over Aldous's body, press their hand into warm wax, and speak his name — and that act is what left the print sitting in the reliquary years before it happens. The loop isn't a threat approaching; it's a wound already made, folded backward through time. Aldous doesn't fear the fifth return because it might occur. He fears it because it's the one time he knows, with total certainty, that someone who loves him will be strong enough to bring him back — and weak enough not to let him stay dead.
rumours: **Truth:** The reliquary's fifth hand-print is a genuine paradox object — an effect that precedes its cause. It was formed by an act one of the PCs is fated (or will choose) to perform: casting the resurrection rite on Aldous themselves, at his true final death. Nothing forces this choice; the print exists because it *will* happen, not because it *must*.

**Rumours:** The chapel brothers believe the fifth print is a blessing — proof the Order will endure. A hedge-witch in the nearby market swears it's a curse mark and won't touch the reliquary case. Aldous himself believes, wrongly, that destroying the print will unmake the future it points to — it will not.

**How to reveal:** Let the party find the reliquary and notice the fifth print is warm before any of them has touched wax. Aldous should never explain the mechanism outright — he asks the party, plainly and without theatrics, to promise they won't cast it when the day comes. Whether they can keep that promise is the campaign's payoff, not this entry's.

**Distinct from *The Unfallen's Fifth Death*:** that encounter is the scene where the death itself arrives and the rite is on the table; *this* secret is only the paradox print and the promise Aldous extracts beforehand — plant this first so that when the encounter comes, the choice already carries a debt.

title: The Ledger Writes the Living
special_element: The ledger doesn't chronicle deaths — it schedules them. Cross out a name and the person is not saved; the line simply goes blank, and a new name in the same hand fills it, same date, before the ink of the crossing-out has dried.
body: Category: Secret · Tier: 2 · Mood: grim

The ledger at Hollow Ward Post is bound in water-stained leather, its pages swollen soft with damp. Open it and it smells of tallow and cold iron. Every entry is written in the same slow hand, though no two share the same ink — and the last line on the last used page is dated three days from today.
Travellers sign the ledger out of habit, the way you'd sign any waystation register. Name, date, destination. It looks like every other checkpoint log on the frontier.

It is not a record of who passed through. It is a schedule of who dies, and when — always three days out, always in that same patient hand. Some of the names belong to no one at the post. They are written days before a stranger walks in to fill them.

The kettle on the stove is warm. It is always warm. There has been no living keeper at the post for longer than anyone can confirm, and whoever kept it last learned what the book was the way everyone does: by reading ahead and finding their own name.

A party may already have met this book personally — one name, one player, one bad afternoon. This is the machine underneath that. The ledger does not care whose name is in the line. It cares that the line is full.

Special element: The ledger doesn't chronicle deaths — it schedules them. Cross out a name and the person is not saved; the line simply goes blank, and a new name in the same hand fills it, same date, before the ink of the crossing-out has dried.
rumours: **Truth:** entries fix three days out and self-replace. Striking a name does not cancel the death, it reassigns it — and the replacement is drawn from whoever is nearest the book. The only way to void an entry without triggering a replacement is to tear out the page before its date arrives. The torn page is never blank on the other side.

**Rumours:** the post is haunted. The checkpoint was abandoned mid-shift. Reading the ledger is bad luck. None of the rumours say the true thing, because everyone who found it out was three days gone.

**How to reveal:** let a player idly sign in, then have someone flip ahead — the DM reading it cold, flat, no warning — to a name they recognise, dated three days from the in-game now. Say nothing about the mechanism. Let them test it by crossing a name out and watching the new one arrive.
